title: Dog Head Pressing: Urgent Guide to Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Dog pressing their head against a wall? This is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Learn the signs, causes, and why you must act immediately.
content_html:
What is Head Pressing in Dogs?

Head pressing is a compulsive behavior where a dog stands and pushes the top of their head against a wall, furniture, or other hard objects for no apparent reason. It is important to distinguish this from affectionate nudging; in head pressing, the dog appears stuck or distressed and often lacks a sense of purpose or social interaction.

Is This an Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for head pressing is High. This behavior is a hallmark sign of a serious neurological condition or damage to the forebrain. It can indicate life-threatening issues such as lead poisoning, liver failure (hepatic encephalopathy), brain tumors, stroke, or severe inflammation of the brain (encephalitis). If you see your dog doing this, you should seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

When you call or arrive at the emergency clinic, providing a short video of the behavior is incredibly valuable. Neurological symptoms can sometimes be intermittent, and the stress of travel may cause your dog to temporarily stop the behavior. A clear video allows the veterinarian to confirm the diagnostic sign quickly and differentiate it from other issues like ear pain or focal seizures, leading to faster treatment.

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because head pressing indicates a significant disruption in brain function, waiting even a few hours can lead to irreversible damage or death depending on the underlying cause.

faqs.question: Are there other symptoms that accompany head pressing?
faqs.answer: Yes, you may also notice your dog pacing, circling, experiencing vision changes, or appearing unusually lethargic or disoriented.

faqs.question: Is head pressing the same as 'head butting' for attention?
faqs.answer: No. Head butting is usually a social behavior aimed at a person or another animal. Head pressing is a persistent, fixed pressure against an inanimate object, often accompanied by a dazed expression.
